title: Ardabil Carpet
topic_path: Art of the Islamic world 640 to now

assessment_items.question_content:
**Carpets decorated the floors of mosques, shrines and homes, but they could also be hung on walls of houses to preserve warmth in the winter. Those from Iran were highly prized.**


![](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/64fecf64172b5876b7a30ca8724aa1a9.jpg)

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
**Carpets covered the floors of many different kinds of buildings and were used for warmth. Carpets from Iran, known as Persian carpets, were especially prized.**

**[Read this essay to review](/test-prep/ap-art-history/west-and-central-asia/a/later-period-the-ardabil-carpet)**

assessment_items.question_content:
**This carpet, along with its twin, were made for the floor of [[☃ dropdown 1]].**


![](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/64fecf64172b5876b7a30ca8724aa1a9.jpg)
hints:
**This carpet was one of a matching pair that was made for the shrine of Safi al-Din Ardabili when it was enlarged in the late 1530s.**

**[Read this essay to review](/test-prep/ap-art-history/west-and-central-asia/a/later-period-the-ardabil-carpet)**

assessment_items.question_content:
**The Ardabil Carpet is a luxury item because of its high [[☃ dropdown 1]], which allows for a more detailed and elaborate pattern.**


![](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/64fecf64172b5876b7a30ca8724aa1a9.jpg)
hints:
**The knot-count of a carpet directly impacts the value of carpets today; the more knots per square centimeter, the more detailed and elaborate the patterns can be.**

**[Read this essay to review](/test-prep/ap-art-history/west-and-central-asia/a/later-period-the-ardabil-carpet)**

assessment_items.question_content:
**An inscription woven into the carpet indicates that Maqsud Kashani was probably the ruler who both commissioned and paid for the carpet.**

![](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/64fecf64172b5876b7a30ca8724aa1a9.jpg)

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
**Maqsud was probably the court official charged with producing the carpets.**

**[Read this essay to review](/test-prep/ap-art-history/west-and-central-asia/a/later-period-the-ardabil-carpet)**
